behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the real Git rename-detection/output boundary plus CLI parsing. This behavior depends on how Git reports renames in an actual repository (similarity heuristics, status formats), which mocks can misrepresent and thereby hide real misclassification bugs. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text is explicitly about repository change semantics ('Renamed files... not also counted as deletion plus addition'), which are produced by real Git/CLI interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp Git repo, commit a file, rename it, run the CLI as a subprocess, and assert the report shows the file only in `renamed` with no corresponding `added`/`deleted` entries.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: This failure typically appears at real subprocess/encoding boundaries (binary patch output, non-text diff content, Git output flags). In-process stubs often return sanitized text and can miss crashes caused by real binary diff handling. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text explicitly requires real binary diff handling ('include binary files... do not crash').
behaviors.test_description: Create a real Git repo with committed and changed binary files, invoke the CLI process, assert zero crash/exit failure, and verify binary file changes are present in the report counts.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The key failure boundary is real Git behavior for unborn HEAD/no-commit repos (exit codes/messages), handled by the CLI. A fake Git adapter can miss real command failure modes and stderr patterns. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text explicitly targets repository state observed through real Git ('repository that has no commits yet').
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real Git repo with no commits, run the CLI subprocess, and assert successful execution plus an empty structured report instead of an error.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end correctness check against actual Git history semantics, requiring real repository evolution and CLI execution. Mocking Git/history would risk tautological tests that mirror implementation assumptions and miss real mismatches. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text anchors truth to 'actual change history of the repository,' which is a real-system boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real commit history containing added/deleted/renamed/modified files across multiple commits, run the CLI, and compare reported counts to independently computed ground truth from Git commands.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real Git index vs working-tree state and how the CLI queries each state. Fakes often collapse these states and can hide real divergences. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text explicitly contrasts real staging states ('staged or unstaged'), which are Git/OS-backed state boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: In a real Git repo, create equivalent file changes once as unstaged and once staged, run the CLI in both cases, and assert byte-for-byte identical structured output.
